enrichment.short_description: Lu.ma (Luma) is an events and ticketing platform for creating, promoting, and managing online and in-person events, meetups, and communities.
enrichment.seo_title: Lu.ma Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Lu.ma (Luma) review 2026 covering event ticketing and community features, Free vs Plus pricing, pros, cons, and top alternatives for event organizers.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Lu.ma, commonly written and referred to as Luma, is an events and community platform founded in 2020 by Victor Pontis alongside a small founding engineering team, initially operating out of New York City. The product began as a lightweight way to create polished event pages with RSVP and calendar tools, and grew during the pandemic-era boom in virtual events before pivoting toward hybrid and in-person gatherings as restrictions lifted. Luma raised a 3 million dollar seed round in December 2020 led by investors including Maven Ventures and Venrock, and in 2024 closed a 12 million dollar Series A that funded expansion to roughly 50 employees, the opening of a San Francisco hub, and the build-out of enterprise CRM integrations. By early 2026 the platform reportedly powers more than 1.5 million events annually and has become deeply embedded in the technology, venture capital, and artificial intelligence conference circuits, hosting recurring communities such as The AI Collective and large-scale programs like the AI+ Renaissance conference series. It is important to distinguish Lu.ma from two other companies that share the Luma name: Luma AI (lumalabs.ai), a generative AI video and 3D company behind Dream Machine, and Luma Health, a healthcare patient engagement platform. All three are unrelated businesses that happen to use similar branding. Architecturally, Lu.ma is a web-based SaaS product centered on an event page builder, a public and private calendar system for organizing recurring communities, guest management with waitlists and approval flows, integrated payments and ticketing, check-in tools via a companion mobile app, and native Zoom integration for hybrid or virtual sessions. Hosts can customize registration questions, send email and push reminders to guests, and issue tickets with QR codes for door scanning. The calendar feature differentiates Luma from transaction-first competitors such as Eventbrite by letting organizers build a persistent, followable community hub rather than a one-off event listing, which is a major reason it has become popular with recurring meetup series, hackathons, and demo nights. Pricing follows a freemium model. The free tier supports unlimited events and guests, up to 500 weekly invite sends, guest check-in, and payment processing with a 5 percent platform fee on paid tickets, along with up to three calendar admins. The Plus plan is 59 dollars per month billed annually and removes the platform fee, raises the weekly invite allowance to 5,000 sends, adds tax collection, custom event URLs, five calendar admins, API access, and priority support; additional weekly send capacity can be purchased in tiers up to 100,000 sends per week. An Enterprise tier offers custom pricing for organizations that need single sign-on, advanced integrations, and additional security controls. Luma's target audience spans independent event organizers, community builders, startup and venture capital teams running recurring meetups, conference producers, and creators monetizing workshops or webinars. Its closest competitors include Eventbrite, which remains the dominant player for large-scale ticketed events, Meetup, which focuses on recurring interest-based groups, and newer social-first tools like Partiful that target casual social gatherings rather than professional or community events. Luma differentiates itself through design quality, a calendar-first community model, tight integration with the Zoom and payments ecosystem, and outsized organic adoption within the tech and AI startup world, which has made it something of a default choice for demo days, hackathons, and investor-adjacent gatherings.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Lu.ma is an events and ticketing platform that lets organizers build branded event pages, manage guest lists, sell tickets, and run recurring community calendars. Founded in 2020 and now headquartered in San Francisco, it has grown into one of the most widely used tools for tech, startup, and AI community events.

Unlike transaction-first ticketing sites, Luma emphasizes a persistent calendar that followers can subscribe to, turning a single event into an ongoing community relationship between host and audience.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Core functionality includes customizable event pages, RSVP and waitlist management, integrated payments and ticketing with QR-code check-in, a companion check-in app, native Zoom integration for virtual and hybrid sessions, and shareable public or private calendars for recurring communities.
Hosts can collect custom registration questions, send automated email and push reminders, issue discount codes, and, on paid plans, connect the platform via API to internal CRM and marketing tools.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Luma's Free plan supports unlimited events and guests with a 5 percent fee on paid ticket sales. The Plus plan, at 59 dollars per month billed annually, removes that fee and unlocks higher invite volumes, custom URLs, tax collection, and API access.
Larger organizations can move to a custom-priced Enterprise tier with single sign-on and advanced security and integration options.

features.title: Event pages
features.description: Branded, customizable landing pages for each event with cover images, descriptions, and registration forms.

features.title: Community calendars
features.description: Public or private calendars that let hosts build a followable community rather than a one-off listing.

features.title: Ticketing and payments
features.description: Built-in payment processing for paid events, with configurable fees, discount codes, and tax collection on paid plans.

features.title: Guest check-in app
features.description: A companion mobile app for scanning QR-code tickets and managing door check-in in real time.

features.title: Zoom integration
features.description: Native connection to Zoom for hosting virtual or hybrid sessions directly from an event page.

features.title: Automated communications
features.description: Scheduled email and push reminders to registered guests before and after events.

features.title: API access
features.description: Programmatic access to event and guest data for CRM and marketing integrations on Plus and Enterprise plans.

features.title: Multi-admin calendar management
features.description: Support for multiple team members to co-manage a calendar and its events.

pros: Polished, fast event page creation with minimal setup
Calendar model builds a durable community following instead of one-off ticket sales
Free tier is genuinely usable for unlimited events
Strong adoption within the tech, startup, and AI event ecosystem
Simple, well-designed check-in flow via companion app
cons: 5 percent platform fee on paid tickets unless upgraded to Plus
Less mature for very large-scale ticketed events compared to Eventbrite
Enterprise features like SSO require custom, undisclosed pricing
Limited built-in marketing/promotion tools compared to dedicated event marketing suites
Weekly invite send limits can require add-on purchases for high-volume communities

faqs.question: Is Lu.ma free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes, Luma offers a free plan with unlimited events and guests, though paid tickets incur a 5 percent platform fee unless the host upgrades to the Plus plan.

faqs.question: What is the difference between Lu.ma and Luma AI?
faqs.answer: They are unrelated companies. Lu.ma is an events and ticketing platform, while Luma AI, at lumalabs.ai, is a separate company that makes AI video and 3D generation tools such as Dream Machine.

faqs.question: Does Lu.ma support virtual and hybrid events?
faqs.answer: Yes, Luma has native Zoom integration so hosts can run virtual or hybrid sessions directly from an event page.

faqs.question: How much does Lu.ma Plus cost?
faqs.answer: Luma Plus costs 59 dollars per month when billed annually, and removes the 5 percent platform ticket fee while adding higher invite limits, tax collection, and API access.

faqs.question: Can I sell tickets on Lu.ma?
faqs.answer: Yes, Luma includes built-in payment processing for paid ticketed events on both the Free and Plus plans.

faqs.question: Who uses Lu.ma?
faqs.answer: Luma is used by independent event organizers, startup and venture capital teams, conference producers, and community builders, and has become especially popular for recurring tech and AI meetups and hackathons.

use_cases.title: Recurring community meetups
use_cases.description: Organizers build a followable calendar to host regular meetups, workshops, or demo nights under one branded community hub.

use_cases.title: Startup and conference events
use_cases.description: Startups and conference producers use Luma to sell tickets, manage guest lists, and check in attendees for in-person or hybrid events.

use_cases.title: Virtual workshops and webinars
use_cases.description: Creators run paid or free virtual sessions through Luma's Zoom integration, collecting registrations and automating reminders.
use_cases.audience: Creators and educators hosting online sessions

enrichment.short_description: Luanti is a free, open-source voxel game engine and sandbox platform, formerly known as Minetest, renamed in October 2024.
enrichment.seo_title: Luanti Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Luanti (formerly Minetest) review 2026: open-source voxel engine features, modding with Lua, pricing, pros, cons, and alternatives like Minecraft.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Luanti is a free and open-source voxel game creation engine that was originally released in October 2010 as Minetest, created by Finnish developer Perttu Ahola as an exploration of the block-building mechanics popularized by Minecraft. Over more than a decade of community-driven development, the project evolved from a single Minecraft-like game into a general-purpose engine capable of powering many distinct voxel games and mods, which made the original name increasingly inaccurate and, according to the project team, a source of confusion and mild embarrassment because of the word test in the name. On October 13, 2024, the project was officially renamed to Luanti, a name that combines Lua, the scripting language the engine is built around, with the Finnish word luonti, meaning creation. The rename was the result of years of community brainstorming and was intended to signal that the software is a general engine for building voxel games rather than a single Minecraft clone; the original Minetest game itself continues to exist as the default bundled game within the Luanti engine. Luanti is maintained by a volunteer community referred to as the Luanti Team, with development happening in the open on GitHub under the organization luanti-org, and it has no traditional company structure, headquarters, or commercial funding rounds; it is sustained through open-source contributions and voluntary donations. The engine's source code is released under the MIT license, allowing free commercial and non-commercial use, modification, and redistribution, while game content and media assets are generally distributed under Creative Commons licenses such as CC BY-SA 3.0, and iconography from Font Awesome is used under CC BY 4.0. Technically, Luanti provides an enormous playable world of up to 62,000 by 62,000 by 62,000 blocks, with mining and building extending roughly 31,000 blocks in each vertical direction, and it supports single-player, local multiplayer, and dedicated online server play. Its defining feature is a comprehensive Lua application programming interface that lets creators write custom games, mods, and map generators without touching the engine's C++ core, and the ContentDB repository hosts more than 3,000 community-created mods, games, and texture packs that can be installed directly from within the client. Luanti runs natively on Windows, macOS, GNU/Linux, BSD variants, and Android, making it broadly cross-platform and lightweight enough to run on modest hardware, which distinguishes it from proprietary voxel games that require more powerful systems or subscription-based server hosting. Because it costs nothing to download, modify, or self-host, Luanti's primary audience includes hobbyist game developers learning Lua scripting, educators using voxel worlds for teaching programming concepts, retro and open-source gaming communities, and players seeking a Minecraft-like experience without licensing costs or proprietary restrictions. Its closest comparison point is Minecraft, from which it draws its core gameplay inspiration, but Luanti differentiates itself by being fully open source, free of any purchase price or subscription, and architected from the ground up as a moddable engine rather than a single fixed game, giving developers far more latitude to build entirely custom voxel experiences. Other comparison points include other open-source or community-driven sandbox projects, though Luanti has one of the longest continuous development histories and largest mod ecosystems in that space, with releases continuing on a regular cadence, including version 5.16.1 in 2026.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Luanti is a free, open-source voxel game engine originally launched in 2010 as Minetest and renamed in October 2024. It lets anyone play, mod, or build entirely new block-based games using a Lua scripting API.
The project is community-maintained with no company or headquarters, distributed under the MIT license for its code and Creative Commons licenses for game content.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Luanti supports single-player, local multiplayer, and dedicated server play across a massive voxel world, with a Lua API that exposes nearly every part of the engine for custom games and mods.
The ContentDB repository hosts thousands of community mods, games, and texture packs, and the engine runs natively on Windows, macOS, Linux, BSD, and Android.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Luanti is entirely free to download, use, modify, and redistribute under its open-source license, with no paid tiers, subscriptions, or in-app purchases from the core project.
Community-run servers and some third-party content may carry their own separate costs, but the official engine and client remain free.

features.title: Lua modding API
features.description: A comprehensive scripting interface for building custom games, mods, and mechanics without modifying the engine's C++ core.

features.title: Massive voxel worlds
features.description: Playable maps up to 62,000 by 62,000 by 62,000 blocks with deep vertical mining and building space.

features.title: ContentDB mod repository
features.description: An in-client repository with over 3,000 community-made mods, games, and texture packs.

features.title: Cross-platform support
features.description: Native builds for Windows, macOS, GNU/Linux, BSD, and Android.

features.title: Multiplayer and dedicated servers
features.description: Support for local co-op play and self-hosted or community dedicated servers.

features.title: Custom map generation
features.description: Configurable and moddable world generators for building unique terrain types and biomes.

features.title: Open licensing
features.description: MIT-licensed engine code with CC-licensed game assets, permitting free commercial and non-commercial use.

features.title: Active volunteer development
features.description: Regular releases maintained by the Luanti Team and open-source contributors on GitHub.

pros: Completely free and open source with no purchase price
Extremely deep modding and customization via Lua
Large, active library of community mods and games via ContentDB
Runs on modest hardware across five major platforms including Android
No corporate ownership, subscription, or licensing restrictions
cons: Visuals and polish generally lag behind commercial titles like Minecraft
No official company support channel, only community and volunteer maintainers
Rebrand from Minetest to Luanti in 2024 can still cause naming confusion
Mod quality and documentation vary widely across ContentDB
Steeper learning curve for building custom games compared to using the engine to just play

faqs.question: Is Luanti the same as Minetest?
faqs.answer: Yes, Luanti is the new name for Minetest. The project was renamed on October 13, 2024, combining Lua, its scripting language, with the Finnish word for creation, luonti.

faqs.question: Is Luanti free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes, Luanti is completely free, open-source software licensed under MIT for its code and Creative Commons licenses for most game content.

faqs.question: Can I create my own games with Luanti?
faqs.answer: Yes, Luanti exposes a full Lua API that lets developers build entirely custom games, mods, and map generators on top of the engine.

faqs.question: What platforms does Luanti support?
faqs.answer: Luanti runs natively on Windows, macOS, GNU/Linux, BSD systems, and Android.

faqs.question: Is Luanti similar to Minecraft?
faqs.answer: Luanti draws inspiration from Minecraft's voxel building and mining mechanics but is a fully open-source, moddable engine rather than a single proprietary game.

faqs.question: Who maintains Luanti?
faqs.answer: Luanti is maintained by a volunteer community known as the Luanti Team, with development conducted openly on GitHub under the luanti-org organization.

use_cases.title: Custom voxel game development
use_cases.description: Developers use Luanti's Lua API to build entirely original voxel games distinct from the bundled Minetest game.

use_cases.title: Educational programming projects
use_cases.description: Educators use Luanti's moddable, free engine to teach Lua scripting and game logic concepts in classroom settings.

use_cases.title: Free community sandbox play
use_cases.description: Players run or join community servers for cooperative building and survival gameplay without licensing costs.

enrichment.short_description: LubeLogger is a free, open-source, self-hosted web app for tracking vehicle maintenance, service records, and fuel mileage.
enrichment.company_size: Small independent developer / open-source contributor team
enrichment.seo_title: LubeLogger Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: LubeLogger review 2026: self-hosted open-source vehicle maintenance tracker features, setup, pricing, pros, cons, and alternatives compared.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: LubeLogger is a free and open-source, self-hosted vehicle maintenance and fuel mileage tracking application developed by Hargata Softworks, a small software outfit based in Price, Utah, and published under the developer handle hargata on GitHub. The project, hosted in the hargata/lubelog repository, gained visibility in the self-hosting and home-lab community in early 2024 after being highlighted on Hacker News and community forums like Lemmy for its combination of thorough functionality and its unconventional name, which plays on the automotive term lube job alongside the familiar logger suffix used by many self-hosted tracking tools. As an open-source project, LubeLogger is licensed under the MIT license, permitting free personal and commercial use, modification, and redistribution, and it has no venture funding, company headcount, or paid corporate structure in the traditional sense; it is sustained through community contributions, GitHub stars, which number in the thousands, and voluntary support. LubeLogger is distributed primarily as a Docker container image, published to the GitHub Container Registry, and also as a Windows standalone executable, making it straightforward to deploy on home servers, NAS devices, or self-hosting platforms such as Umbrel. The core architecture is a web application with a backend API, meaning users interact with it through a browser after deploying the container on their own infrastructure, which keeps vehicle and maintenance data entirely under the user's control rather than stored on a third-party cloud service. Functionally, LubeLogger provides a dashboard summarizing expenses by year, month, and category; a planner for organizing upcoming maintenance to-do items by type and priority; detailed service record logging with support for attaching receipts and documents; fuel economy tracking that supports multiple units including MPG, UK MPG, liters per 100 kilometers, and kilometers per liter; reminders for scheduled maintenance triggered by either calendar date or odometer mileage; and a supplies tracker for logging consumable inventory like oil or filters tied to specific vehicles. Additional capabilities include generation of professional-looking vehicle service reports suitable for resale documentation, support for multiple user accounts on a single instance, user-customizable fields for adapting the data model to unusual vehicle types, CSV import and export for migrating data in or out of the system, a documented API for integrations, and single sign-on support for users who want to tie authentication into an existing identity provider. LubeLogger's target audience is primarily technically inclined vehicle owners, self-hosting and home-lab enthusiasts, and small fleet or multi-vehicle households who want a private, ad-free alternative to consumer apps like Fuelly, Simply Auto, or CARFAX Car Care, all of which typically store data in a vendor cloud and may carry subscription costs or advertising. Because LubeLogger requires users to run their own server or container, its differentiation is squarely on data ownership, privacy, and zero licensing cost rather than convenience, which appeals to the same audience that gravitates toward other self-hosted tools in categories like home media servers or password managers. The project maintains an active public demo instance at demo.lubelogger.com for prospective users to try the interface before committing to self-hosting, and ongoing development is tracked through frequent GitHub releases that have steadily added features such as reminders, reporting, and API access since the tool's initial public releases.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: LubeLogger is a free, open-source, self-hosted application for tracking vehicle service history, maintenance planning, and fuel economy. It is developed by Hargata Softworks and distributed primarily as a Docker container.
Because it is self-hosted, all vehicle and maintenance data stays on infrastructure the user controls, distinguishing it from cloud-based consumer car maintenance apps.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: The app includes an expense dashboard, a maintenance planner, detailed service record logging with document attachments, multi-unit fuel economy tracking, date- or mileage-based reminders, and a supplies tracker.
It also supports multiple user accounts, customizable fields, CSV import and export, an API, single sign-on, and professional vehicle report generation.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: LubeLogger is completely free and open source under the MIT license, with no subscription fees, feature paywalls, or advertising.
Users only incur the cost of the infrastructure they choose to self-host it on, such as a home server or a low-cost VPS.

features.title: Service record tracking
features.description: Log detailed maintenance and repair history per vehicle, with the ability to attach receipts and documents.

features.title: Fuel economy tracking
features.description: Records fuel entries and calculates mileage in MPG, UK MPG, L/100KM, or KM/L.

features.title: Maintenance reminders
features.description: Set reminders based on either a calendar date or an odometer mileage threshold.

features.title: Expense dashboard
features.description: Summarizes vehicle-related spending by year, month, and category.

features.title: Multi-user and SSO support
features.description: Supports multiple accounts on one instance plus single sign-on for centralized authentication.

features.title: CSV import/export and API
features.description: Move data in or out of the app via CSV files or integrate with other tools through a documented API.

features.title: Supplies tracking
features.description: Tracks consumable inventory like oil, filters, or parts tied to specific vehicles.

features.title: Professional vehicle reports
features.description: Generates polished service history reports useful for vehicle resale or documentation.

pros: Completely free and open source with no subscription cost
Full data ownership through self-hosting
Detailed maintenance, fuel, and supplies tracking in one app
Active development with frequent GitHub releases
Docker-based deployment makes setup straightforward for self-hosters
cons: Requires self-hosting technical know-how such as Docker or a home server
No official cloud-hosted version for non-technical users
No dedicated mobile app, relies on the web interface
Support is community-driven rather than a commercial help desk
Smaller ecosystem and brand recognition than mainstream consumer car apps

faqs.question: Is LubeLogger free?
faqs.answer: Yes, LubeLogger is completely free and open source under the MIT license, with no subscription fees or paywalled features.

faqs.question: Do I need to self-host LubeLogger?
faqs.answer: Yes, LubeLogger is a self-hosted application distributed as a Docker container or Windows executable, so users deploy it on their own server or device.

faqs.question: Can multiple people use one LubeLogger instance?
faqs.answer: Yes, LubeLogger supports multiple user accounts on a single instance, including single sign-on for centralized authentication.

faqs.question: Can I try LubeLogger before installing it?
faqs.answer: Yes, a public demo instance is available at demo.lubelogger.com to test the interface before self-hosting.

faqs.question: What units does LubeLogger support for fuel tracking?
faqs.answer: LubeLogger supports MPG, UK MPG, liters per 100 kilometers, and kilometers per liter for fuel economy calculations.

faqs.question: Who develops LubeLogger?
faqs.answer: LubeLogger is developed by Hargata Softworks, based in Price, Utah, and is published as open source on GitHub under the hargata/lubelog repository.

use_cases.title: Personal vehicle maintenance tracking
use_cases.description: Individual car owners log service history, fuel economy, and maintenance reminders for one or more personal vehicles.

use_cases.title: Self-hosted home-lab deployment
use_cases.description: Home-lab and self-hosting enthusiasts run LubeLogger alongside other self-hosted apps on a home server or NAS.

use_cases.title: Small fleet or household multi-vehicle tracking
use_cases.description: Households or small operators with several vehicles use multi-user support to track maintenance across a small fleet.
use_cases.audience: Small fleet operators and multi-vehicle households

enrichment.short_description: Lucky Orange is a website heatmap, session recording, and conversion analytics tool that helps teams see and fix visitor drop-off.
enrichment.seo_title: Lucky Orange Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Lucky Orange review 2026: heatmaps, session recordings, surveys, and Discovery AI features, pricing tiers, pros, cons, and top competitor comparisons.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Lucky Orange is a website behavior analytics platform that combines dynamic heatmaps, session recordings, conversion funnels, on-site surveys, live chat, and an AI-assisted insights feature called Discovery AI into a single conversion-rate-optimization suite. The company was founded in 2010, with reporting attributing its founding to Brian Gruber, and it is headquartered in Overland Park, Kansas, a notable detail because it places Lucky Orange outside the Silicon Valley-centric hubs where many competing analytics tools are based. Unlike many SaaS analytics companies that raise venture capital, Lucky Orange has described itself as a bootstrapped business that grew its recurring revenue without outside institutional funding, which has shaped a product strategy focused on straightforward pricing and broad feature access rather than aggressive enterprise upselling. The platform is used by more than 500,000 websites worldwide, according to company claims, spanning e-commerce stores, SaaS marketing sites, and content publishers who want to understand how visitors actually interact with their pages beyond what standard traffic analytics like Google Analytics can show. Architecturally, Lucky Orange is a JavaScript tracking snippet installed on a website that captures anonymized visitor interaction data, including mouse movement, clicks, scroll depth, and form field engagement, which is then rendered as aggregate heatmaps or replayed as individual session recordings inside the Lucky Orange dashboard. The conversion funnels feature visualizes where visitors abandon a defined multi-step process, such as a checkout flow, while form analytics isolates which specific fields cause hesitation or abandonment. Surveys and announcements let site owners collect qualitative feedback or broadcast messages directly on their pages, and the live chat and live visitor view features allow support or sales teams to see and message visitors currently browsing the site in real time. Discovery AI is Lucky Orange's newer artificial-intelligence layer, designed to automatically surface behavioral patterns and friction points from recordings and heatmap data rather than requiring a human analyst to manually review every session. Pricing is structured around monthly tracked session volume rather than a strict per-seat or per-feature model, which is a meaningful differentiator: every plan, from the entry-level tier up through the largest, includes the full feature set, including Discovery AI, heatmaps, recordings, surveys, live chat, funnels, and unlimited team members, with the only variable being how many monthly sessions are tracked and how long data is retained. As of the most recent published pricing, the Build plan starts at 32 dollars per month for up to 3,500 monthly sessions, the Grow plan is 72 dollars per month for up to 10,000 sessions and is marketed as the most popular tier, the Expand plan is 199 dollars per month starting at 50,000 sessions, the Scale plan is 839 dollars per month starting at 300,000 sessions, and an Enterprise tier with custom pricing covers sites with 700,000 or more monthly sessions. Base data retention starts at 60 days, with paid upgrades available to extend retention to 90, 180, or 365 days, and additional websites can be added to an account for 5 dollars per site per month. A 7-day free trial requiring no credit card is available across plans. Lucky Orange's target audience is primarily small to mid-market e-commerce and marketing teams, particularly those running Shopify or BigCommerce stores, given its native app listings on both platforms, as well as SaaS companies optimizing onboarding and signup flows. Its core competitors include Hotjar, Microsoft Clarity, which is free and has pressured pricing across the category, Crazy Egg, and FullStory for more enterprise-focused session replay. Lucky Orange differentiates itself by bundling nearly every CRO tool, heatmaps, recordings, surveys, chat, and funnels, into every pricing tier without feature gating, along with its bootstrapped, non-VC-backed positioning and long operating history dating back to 2010.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Lucky Orange is a conversion rate optimization suite offering heatmaps, session recordings, funnels, surveys, live chat, and AI-driven behavioral insights. Founded in 2010 and based in Overland Park, Kansas, it serves over 500,000 websites.
The company has positioned itself as a bootstrapped, self-funded business rather than a venture-backed startup, which shapes its all-features-included pricing approach.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: The platform tracks visitor clicks, scrolling, and mouse movement to generate dynamic heatmaps, and records individual visitor sessions for replay, alongside form analytics that isolate friction-causing fields.
Conversion funnels visualize multi-step drop-off, Discovery AI surfaces patterns automatically, and live chat with live visitor view lets teams engage visitors in real time.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Pricing scales by monthly tracked sessions rather than by feature tier, so every plan from Build through Scale includes the full feature set including Discovery AI.
Plans start at 32 dollars per month for 3,500 sessions and scale to custom Enterprise pricing for sites exceeding 700,000 monthly sessions, with a 7-day free trial available.

features.title: Dynamic heatmaps
features.description: Visualizes where visitors click, how far they scroll, and where they move their mouse on any page.

features.title: Session recordings
features.description: Replays individual visitor sessions to see exactly how users navigate and interact with a site.

features.title: Conversion funnels
features.description: Tracks multi-step processes like checkout to identify where visitors abandon.

features.title: Form analytics
features.description: Identifies which specific form fields cause hesitation or abandonment.

features.title: Discovery AI
features.description: An AI layer that automatically surfaces behavioral patterns and friction points from recordings and heatmaps.

features.title: Live chat and live visitor view
features.description: Lets teams message visitors currently browsing the site and watch their activity in real time.

features.title: Surveys and announcements
features.description: Collects on-site visitor feedback or broadcasts messages directly on pages.

features.title: Unlimited team members
features.description: All plans allow unlimited team seats regardless of pricing tier.

pros: Every plan includes the full feature set, including Discovery AI, with no feature gating
Native Shopify and BigCommerce app integrations for e-commerce teams
Bootstrapped company with a long, stable operating history since 2010
Straightforward session-based pricing that is easy to forecast
7-day free trial with no credit card required
cons: Pricing scales quickly for high-traffic sites once past entry tiers
Base data retention of 60 days requires a paid upgrade for longer history
Competes against free tools like Microsoft Clarity on core heatmap and recording features
Additional websites cost extra per site
Less enterprise-focused depth than dedicated tools like FullStory for very large organizations

faqs.question: How much does Lucky Orange cost?
faqs.answer: Plans start at 32 dollars per month for up to 3,500 monthly sessions when billed annually, scaling up to custom Enterprise pricing for sites with 700,000 or more sessions.

faqs.question: Does Lucky Orange offer a free trial?
faqs.answer: Yes, Lucky Orange offers a 7-day free trial across its plans with no credit card required.

faqs.question: What is Discovery AI in Lucky Orange?
faqs.answer: Discovery AI is Lucky Orange's AI feature that automatically surfaces behavioral patterns and friction points from session recordings and heatmap data.

faqs.question: Is Lucky Orange available for Shopify?
faqs.answer: Yes, Lucky Orange has a native Shopify app as well as a BigCommerce integration for e-commerce heatmaps and session recordings.

faqs.question: Are all features included on every Lucky Orange plan?
faqs.answer: Yes, every paid plan includes the same full feature set, heatmaps, recordings, surveys, live chat, and Discovery AI, differing only by tracked monthly session volume.

faqs.question: How long does Lucky Orange store visitor data?
faqs.answer: Base plans include 60 days of data retention, with paid upgrades available to extend retention to 90, 180, or 365 days.

use_cases.title: E-commerce conversion optimization
use_cases.description: Shopify and BigCommerce store owners use heatmaps and funnels to find checkout friction and abandoned cart causes.
use_cases.audience: E-commerce store owners and CRO teams

use_cases.title: SaaS onboarding analysis

enrichment.short_description: Luma AI is a generative AI company behind Dream Machine, building text-to-video, image, and 3D generation models, unrelated to Lu.ma or Luma Health.
enrichment.seo_title: Luma AI Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Luma AI review 2026: Dream Machine, Ray3, Photon, and Genie 3D features, pricing plans, funding, pros, cons, and alternatives compared.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Luma AI, operating from lumalabs.ai, is a generative artificial intelligence company founded in 2021 by Amit Jain, Alex Yu, and Alberto Taiuti, headquartered in Palo Alto, California. It should not be confused with two other unrelated companies that use similar Luma branding: Lu.ma, an events and ticketing platform at lu.ma, and Luma Health, a healthcare patient engagement company. Luma AI's stated mission is to build multimodal general intelligence models that can generate, understand, and operate within representations of the physical world, and its work spans AI-generated video, images, and 3D assets. The company built an early reputation in neural radiance fields and 3D capture technology before pivoting its primary public focus toward generative video with the June 2024 launch of Dream Machine, a text-to-video and image-to-video web application that quickly attracted a large consumer and creative-professional user base; by early 2026 Dream Machine reportedly had more than 30 million registered users and generated approximately 21.2 million dollars in revenue during 2025. Luma AI has raised more than 1.07 billion dollars in disclosed funding across multiple rounds from investors including Amazon, General Catalyst, NVentures, Amplify Partners, Andreessen Horowitz, and AMD Ventures. Its largest and most recent round was a 900 million dollar Series C announced in November 2025, led by Saudi Arabia's HUMAIN and valuing the company above 4 billion dollars, which also established Project Halo, a planned 2-gigawatt AI supercomputing cluster to be built in Saudi Arabia in partnership with HUMAIN. The company has grown rapidly alongside this funding, with employee counts reported in the low hundreds through 2025 and continuing expansion into new offices in London and Riyadh as of 2026. Luma AI's product suite is organized around several distinct models accessible both through its consumer-facing web apps and its developer API, which launched in September 2024. Dream Machine remains the flagship product for text-to-video, image-to-video, video-to-video, and video extension generation. Ray2 and the newer Ray3, released in September 2025 and marketed as the first reasoning video model capable of generating high-fidelity 16-bit HDR output, represent the underlying video generation architecture and are available at different speed and quality tiers, including a faster and cheaper Ray2 Flash variant. Photon and Photon Flash are Luma's image generation models, built for fast, high-quality image synthesis. Genie is a text-to-3D tool that generates downloadable 3D mesh files in formats including GLB, OBJ, and USDZ in roughly ten seconds, aimed at game developers, 3D artists, and virtual production teams. Consumer pricing is organized into Plus, Pro, and Ultra subscription tiers, priced at approximately 30, 90, and 300 dollars per month respectively when billed monthly, with discounted annual pricing offering up to 20 percent savings; each tier provides an increasing monthly credit allowance, ranging from roughly 10,000 credits on Plus to 150,000 credits on Ultra, along with escalating usage limits for Luma's automated agent tooling. Business-oriented Team and Enterprise plans are available at custom pricing and add team management, shared credit pools, usage analytics, single sign-on, and for Enterprise customers, custom model fine-tuning. API pricing for developers is usage-based, with reported costs of roughly 0.10 dollars per five-second clip for Ray2 Flash, 0.40 dollars for full Ray2, 0.002 dollars per image for Photon Flash, and 0.05 dollars per mesh for Genie. Luma AI's target audience includes independent creators and marketers who use Dream Machine for short-form video content, game and 3D artists using Genie for rapid asset prototyping, and enterprise and developer customers integrating Luma's models via API into larger creative or production pipelines. Its primary competitors in generative video include Runway, OpenAI's Sora, Google's Veo, Pika, and Kling, while in 3D generation it competes with tools like Meshy and Tripo; Luma differentiates itself through its focus on physically grounded, reasoning-capable video generation and its parallel investment in 3D and spatial intelligence rather than video alone.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Luma AI is a generative AI company founded in 2021 and based in Palo Alto, California, best known for Dream Machine, its text-to-video and image-to-video web application launched in June 2024.
It is a completely separate company from Lu.ma, the events and ticketing platform, and Luma Health, the healthcare patient engagement company, despite the shared Luma name.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: The product suite includes Dream Machine for video generation, the Ray2 and Ray3 reasoning video models, Photon and Photon Flash for fast image generation, and Genie for text-to-3D mesh generation.
Models are accessible through both consumer web apps and a developer API launched in September 2024, with usage-based pricing for API access.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Consumer plans include Plus, Pro, and Ultra subscription tiers priced from roughly 25 to 300 dollars per month depending on billing cycle, each with escalating monthly credit allowances.
Team and Enterprise plans are custom-priced and add SSO, shared credits, and fine-tuning, while API usage is billed per generation.

features.description: Custom model fine-tuning and enterprise commitments available on the Enterprise plan.

Broad product suite spanning video, image, and 3D generation
Well-funded with over 1 billion dollars raised, supporting rapid model development

Genie offers fast, practical 3D asset generation for games and prototyping
cons: Credit-based consumer pricing can be difficult to estimate for heavy users
Business and Enterprise pricing is not publicly disclosed
Faces intense competition from well-funded rivals like OpenAI Sora, Google Veo, and Runway
Frequent name confusion with unrelated companies Lu.ma and Luma Health
Rapid model iteration can make older outputs and workflows quickly outdated

faqs.question: Is Luma AI the same company as Lu.ma?
faqs.answer: No, they are completely unrelated companies. Luma AI at lumalabs.ai builds generative AI video, image, and 3D tools, while Lu.ma is a separate events and ticketing platform.

faqs.question: What is Dream Machine?
faqs.answer: Dream Machine is Luma AI's text-to-video and image-to-video web application, launched in June 2024, that lets users generate video clips from prompts, images, or existing footage.

faqs.question: How much does Luma AI cost?
faqs.answer: Consumer plans start around 25 to 30 dollars per month for the Plus tier, scaling to roughly 300 dollars per month for Ultra, with custom pricing for Team and Enterprise plans.

faqs.question: What is Genie in Luma AI?
faqs.answer: Genie is Luma AI's text-to-3D tool that generates downloadable 3D mesh files in formats like GLB, OBJ, and USDZ in about ten seconds.

faqs.question: How much funding has Luma AI raised?
faqs.answer: Luma AI has raised over 1.07 billion dollars, including a 900 million dollar Series C in November 2025 led by Saudi Arabia's HUMAIN at a valuation above 4 billion dollars.

faqs.question: Does Luma AI offer an API?
faqs.answer: Yes, Luma AI launched a developer API in September 2024 covering its Dream Machine video, Photon image, and Genie 3D models with usage-based pricing.

enrichment.short_description: Luma Health is a healthcare patient engagement and scheduling platform used by health systems to automate outreach and care coordination, unrelated to Lu.ma or Luma AI.
enrichment.seo_title: Luma Health Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Luma Health review 2026: patient engagement and scheduling platform features, custom pricing, funding, pros, cons, and alternatives for healthcare teams.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Luma Health is a healthcare technology company founded in 2015 and headquartered in San Mateo, California, that builds an operational patient engagement platform used by hospitals, health systems, and clinics to automate communication and coordination across the patient journey. It is entirely unrelated to two other companies with similar branding: Lu.ma, the general-purpose events and ticketing platform, and Luma AI, the generative video and 3D company behind Dream Machine. Luma Health describes its mission as unifying every step of the patient journey, from initial scheduling through referrals, reminders, check-in, follow-up, and recall, into a single automated communication layer that sits on top of a health system's existing electronic health record and practice management systems rather than replacing them. The company has raised approximately 156 million dollars in disclosed funding, with its most notable round being a 130 million dollar Series C led by FTV Capital, which the company said would be used to scale internationally and expand its operational AI capabilities for healthcare. Luma Health positions its product as operational AI for healthcare, reflecting a shift in its messaging toward automation and AI-assisted workflows for tasks like appointment scheduling, waitlist management, and patient outreach that traditionally required significant manual staff time from front-desk and call-center teams. Architecturally, Luma Health integrates directly with major electronic health record systems to pull and push scheduling, referral, and patient contact data, then layers automated, multi-channel communication, including text messaging, email, and phone, on top of that data to handle tasks such as appointment reminders, digital intake forms, automated waitlist backfill when a slot opens up, referral scheduling, and post-visit follow-up or recall campaigns for preventive care. The platform is built for large, complex healthcare organizations, and its own marketing describes the product as well suited to larger healthcare organizations and multi-location systems with intricate scheduling and integration requirements, rather than small independent practices. Because Luma Health does not publish pricing publicly, prospective customers must go through a sales consultation in which a representative evaluates the size of the practice or health system, existing patient volume, the specific features required, and the complexity of the EHR integration involved before providing a quote; third-party reviews and cost estimate sites have reported anecdotal pricing figures in the range of roughly 250 dollars per user per month, though Luma Health itself does not confirm this publicly, and actual contract pricing likely varies considerably based on organization size and scope. The company's target audience includes hospital systems, multi-specialty clinics, federally qualified health centers, and other healthcare provider organizations seeking to reduce no-show rates, decrease administrative burden on front-office staff, and improve patient access and satisfaction metrics. Luma Health's primary competitors in the patient engagement and scheduling technology space include Solutionreach, NextGen Healthcare's patient engagement tools, Phreesia, Relatient, and Klara, along with broader healthcare CRM and communication platforms. Luma Health differentiates itself through its EHR-agnostic integration approach, meaning it is designed to layer on top of many different underlying EHR systems rather than being tied to one vendor's ecosystem, and through its more recent emphasis on operational AI, positioning automated decision-making and workflow orchestration, rather than simple reminder messaging, as its core value proposition to health system executives evaluating patient access and revenue-cycle technology investments.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Luma Health is a healthcare patient engagement and scheduling platform founded in 2015 and headquartered in San Mateo, California, used by hospitals and clinics to automate patient communication and care coordination.
It is unrelated to Lu.ma, the events platform, and Luma AI, the generative video company, despite sharing the Luma name.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: The platform integrates with electronic health record systems to automate appointment reminders, digital intake, waitlist backfill, referral scheduling, and post-visit follow-up across text, email, and phone channels.
Luma Health has increasingly positioned its product around operational AI, aiming to automate scheduling and outreach decisions rather than simply sending reminders.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Luma Health does not publish pricing publicly and instead quotes each healthcare organization individually based on size, patient volume, feature needs, and EHR integration complexity.
Third-party estimates suggest costs can run around 250 dollars per user per month, though actual enterprise contract pricing varies significantly by organization.

faqs.question: Is Luma Health the same as Lu.ma or Luma AI?

faqs.question: How much does Luma Health cost?
faqs.answer: Luma Health uses custom, quote-based pricing based on organization size and needs; it does not publish pricing publicly, though some reviews estimate costs around 250 dollars per user per month.

faqs.question: What does Luma Health do?
faqs.answer: Luma Health automates patient engagement tasks such as appointment reminders, digital intake, waitlist backfill, referral scheduling, and follow-up recall for healthcare organizations.

faqs.question: Does Luma Health integrate with EHR systems?
faqs.answer: Yes, Luma Health is built to integrate with a wide range of existing electronic health record and practice management systems.

faqs.answer: Luma Health is primarily used by hospital systems, multi-specialty clinics, and other larger healthcare provider organizations.

enrichment.short_description: Lumar is an enterprise technical SEO and website intelligence platform, formerly known as DeepCrawl, used to crawl, monitor, and protect large websites.
enrichment.seo_title: Lumar Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Lumar (formerly DeepCrawl) review 2026: enterprise SEO crawling and website intelligence features, pricing, pros, cons, and alternatives like Botify.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Lumar is an enterprise technical SEO and website intelligence platform that was originally founded in 2010 in the United Kingdom under the name DeepCrawl. The company built its early reputation as a large-scale website crawler purpose-built for technical SEO teams at enterprise organizations, distinguishing itself from consumer or small-business SEO tools by focusing on the crawling and auditing needs of very large, complex websites with millions of URLs. On September 29, 2022, the company rebranded from DeepCrawl to Lumar, a name the company said was chosen to evoke illumination, reflecting its positioning as a tool that helps organizations see their website's technical foundations clearly in order to uncover new opportunities to drive traffic and conversions. The rebrand accompanied a broader expansion of the product from a narrowly focused technical SEO crawler into what the company describes as a website intelligence platform intended to serve digital marketing and engineering teams more broadly, not just dedicated SEO specialists. Lumar is headquartered in London, England, with additional offices reported in New York and Krakow, Poland; reported employee counts vary meaningfully across data providers, from roughly a dozen up to the low hundreds, likely reflecting differences in how contractors, regional teams, and historical snapshots are counted. Lumar's operating entity is registered as Written Byte Ltd, trading as Lumar, and the company has at various points been associated with ownership or investment from GMO TECH Holdings, a Japan-based, Tokyo Stock Exchange-listed digital marketing group. Product-wise, Lumar is organized around four coordinated applications. Analyze is the core cloud-based crawler, capable of crawling large websites and surfacing more than 350 distinct technical SEO reports covering issues like crawl budget waste, indexability problems, broken links, duplicate content, and site architecture. Monitor provides ongoing, multi-site tracking with trend lines and automated alerts so teams can catch regressions as they happen rather than only during periodic audits. Protect integrates into a customer's CI/CD pipeline to test staging or pre-production builds for SEO regressions before they are deployed to a live site, aiming to prevent costly technical SEO mistakes, like accidental noindex tags or broken canonical URLs, from ever reaching production. Impact rounds out the suite with high-level dashboards and benchmarking data intended for executive stakeholders who need summarized site health and business-impact reporting rather than granular crawl data. Because Lumar targets large enterprise organizations with complex sites, its client base is reported to include major brands such as Adobe, Microsoft, eBay, PayPal, Comcast, and Deloitte, and the platform is generally positioned and priced for organizations with sizable marketing technology budgets rather than freelancers or small businesses. Lumar does not publish pricing publicly; prospective customers must request a custom quote through the company's sales process, with pricing and packaging described by the company as flexible and tailored to the specific combination of Analyze, Monitor, Protect, and Impact modules a customer needs, as well as crawl volume and site count. Lumar's primary competitors in the enterprise technical SEO and site crawling space include Botify, ContentKing (part of Conductor), Screaming Frog for more manual or smaller-scale crawling, and OnCrawl, with Botify generally considered its closest direct rival for large enterprise accounts. Lumar differentiates itself through its long operating history dating back to 2010, its CI/CD-integrated Protect module aimed at preventing SEO regressions before deployment rather than only detecting them afterward, and its broader positioning as a website intelligence platform spanning technical SEO, monitoring, and executive reporting in one coordinated product suite.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Lumar is an enterprise technical SEO and website intelligence platform, founded in 2010 as DeepCrawl and rebranded to Lumar in September 2022. It is built for large organizations managing complex, high-URL-volume websites.
The platform is organized into four modules, Analyze, Monitor, Protect, and Impact, spanning crawling, ongoing monitoring, pre-deploy SEO testing, and executive-level reporting.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Analyze crawls large sites and generates over 350 technical SEO reports, while Monitor tracks multiple sites over time with trend lines and alerts for regressions.
Protect hooks into CI/CD pipelines to catch SEO issues in staging before deployment, and Impact provides executive dashboards summarizing site health and business impact.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Lumar does not publish pricing publicly and instead offers custom, quote-based packages tailored to a customer's chosen modules, crawl volume, and number of sites.
Prospective customers must submit a request through Lumar's sales team to receive pricing, which is typical of enterprise-tier SEO and website intelligence platforms.

Trusted by major brands including Adobe, Microsoft, and PayPal
cons: Pricing is not publicly disclosed, requiring a sales consultation
Positioned for enterprise budgets, likely too costly for small businesses
Reported employee counts vary widely across sources, suggesting limited public transparency
Learning curve for teams new to large-scale technical SEO crawling
Less suited to quick, ad hoc audits than lighter tools like Screaming Frog

faqs.question: Is Lumar the same as DeepCrawl?
faqs.answer: Yes, Lumar is the rebranded name for DeepCrawl, which changed its name on September 29, 2022, while continuing the same core crawling technology and team.

faqs.question: How much does Lumar cost?
faqs.answer: Lumar uses custom, quote-based pricing tailored to a customer's chosen modules, crawl volume, and number of sites, and does not publish pricing publicly.

faqs.question: What modules does Lumar offer?
faqs.answer: Lumar's platform includes Analyze for crawling, Monitor for ongoing multi-site tracking, Protect for CI/CD SEO testing, and Impact for executive dashboards.

faqs.question: Who uses Lumar?
faqs.answer: Lumar is used by enterprise organizations with large, complex websites, including reported clients such as Adobe, Microsoft, eBay, PayPal, Comcast, and Deloitte.

faqs.question: Where is Lumar headquartered?
faqs.answer: Lumar is headquartered in London, United Kingdom, with additional offices reported in New York and Krakow, Poland.

faqs.question: What is Lumar's Protect feature?
faqs.answer: Protect integrates into a customer's CI/CD pipeline to test staging builds for SEO regressions and stop harmful changes before they reach production.

enrichment.short_description: Lunacy is a free, cross-platform graphic and UI design tool by Icons8 with built-in AI tools and design assets, positioned as an alternative to Figma and Sketch.
enrichment.headquarters: New York, United States (Icons8, parent company)
enrichment.seo_title: Lunacy Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Lunacy by Icons8 review 2026: free design tool features, AI tools, pricing plans, pros, cons, and how it compares to Figma and Sketch.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Lunacy is a free graphic and UI design application developed by Icons8, a design-assets company whose broader history traces back to a small team of designers working with clients like Oracle, Motorola, and Europol in the early 2000s before formally incorporating as Icons8 in 2012. Lunacy itself began life in 2016 as a simple Sketch file viewer for Windows, built to solve the problem that Sketch, which had become a default standard design tool, was available exclusively on macOS and left Windows-based designers unable to open or view Sketch files. Early on, Sketch changed its file format shortly after Lunacy's initial release, forcing the team to rewrite much of the underlying code, but the viewer nonetheless became one of the top downloaded design tools in the Microsoft Store. From there, Icons8 progressively built out Lunacy into a full editor, releasing Lunacy 3.0 as a native Windows application capable of opening, editing, and saving true .sketch files, and continuing through subsequent major versions, including Lunacy 5.0, which added an integrated vector editor alongside built-in access to icons, photos, and illustrations, and later versions that expanded to native macOS and Linux clients and introduced AI-powered tools. Icons8, the parent company, is reported to be headquartered in New York with additional distributed team presence, and reported employee counts across various data sources range from roughly 47 to 55 people, making it a small, independently operated company relative to venture-backed design tool competitors like Figma. As a product, Lunacy is a full-featured vector design application supporting artboards, responsive design constraints, interactive prototyping with clickable links between screens, and native compatibility with .sketch files, positioning it as a direct alternative for teams that want Sketch-like or Figma-like workflows without being locked into macOS or a browser-based subscription tool. A defining differentiator is that Lunacy runs as a native desktop application on Windows, macOS, and Linux and can be used entirely offline, in contrast to Figma, which is primarily browser-based and requires an internet connection for most of its functionality. Lunacy also ships with deep integration into Icons8's own asset library, giving users in-app access to more than 1.5 million free icons, photos, illustrations, and mockups directly inside the design canvas, alongside built-in AI tools such as a background remover, an image upscaler, and an AI avatar generator. Pricing follows a freemium structure: the core desktop application and most major design features are free for both personal and commercial use with no feature restrictions on the base editor, while paid add-ons unlock cloud collaboration and asset-related upgrades. A free team plan supports up to three members and ten cloud documents, a personal cloud subscription costs approximately 4.99 dollars per month or 47.99 dollars per year and adds unlimited private cloud documents plus extended file version history and recovery windows, a professional team plan is priced at 4.99 dollars per seat per month with no limits on documents or version history, and a separate asset subscription priced around 9.99 dollars per month or 95.99 dollars per year unlocks unlimited downloads from the Icons8 asset library without attribution requirements plus full access to the AI-powered design tools. Lunacy's target audience includes solo designers, startups, and small teams who want a capable, no-cost design tool, particularly those on Windows or Linux who are otherwise excluded from Sketch's Mac-only ecosystem, as well as users who value offline-first, locally stored design files over cloud-only workflows. Its main competitors are Figma, the dominant browser-based collaborative design tool, Sketch, the Mac-only tool Lunacy originally aimed to complement, Adobe XD, and other emerging free or open-source design tools like Penpot. Lunacy differentiates itself primarily on price, being free with no core feature restrictions, on cross-platform native desktop support including Linux, and on its built-in, no-extra-cost access to a large stock asset library and AI design utilities bundled directly into the editor.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Lunacy is a free, cross-platform graphic and UI design tool made by Icons8. It began in 2016 as a Windows viewer for Sketch files and evolved into a full vector design editor running natively on Windows, macOS, and Linux.

It is positioned as a free alternative to Figma and Sketch, with the added advantage of full offline functionality since it runs as a native desktop application rather than requiring a browser connection.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Lunacy supports artboards, responsive design constraints, interactive prototyping, and native .sketch file compatibility, and it ships with built-in AI tools such as background removal, image upscaling, and avatar generation.
The app includes direct in-canvas access to Icons8's library of over 1.5 million free icons, photos, and illustrations, reducing the need for separate stock asset subscriptions.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: The core Lunacy desktop editor is free for personal and commercial use with no major feature restrictions, distinguishing it from most competitors that gate core functionality behind paywalls.
Paid add-ons cover cloud storage and collaboration, priced from about 4.99 dollars per month, and a separate asset and AI subscription costs roughly 9.99 dollars per month.

features.title: Native cross-platform desktop app
features.description: Runs natively on Windows, macOS, and Linux, and works fully offline without requiring a browser.

features.title: Sketch file compatibility
features.description: Opens, edits, and saves native .sketch files, enabling collaboration with Sketch users on non-Mac systems.

features.title: Built-in AI tools
features.description: Includes an AI background remover, image upscaler, and avatar generator directly inside the editor.

features.title: Integrated asset library
features.description: Direct in-app access to over 1.5 million free icons, photos, illustrations, and mockups from Icons8.

features.title: Interactive prototyping
features.description: Lets designers create clickable prototypes by linking artboards together.

features.title: Responsive design constraints
features.description: Supports layout rules and constraints so designs adapt across different screen sizes.

features.title: Cloud collaboration (paid)
features.description: Optional cloud document storage, team sharing, and version history on paid subscription tiers.

features.title: Vector editing toolkit
features.description: Full vector drawing, editing, and artboard tools comparable to standard UI design software.

pros: Core design editor is free for personal and commercial use with no major feature gating
Native desktop apps for Windows, macOS, and Linux with full offline support
Built-in access to a massive free icon, photo, and illustration library
Includes AI tools like background removal and upscaling at no extra base cost
Native .sketch file compatibility helps teams collaborate across platforms
cons: Smaller collaborative and plugin ecosystem than Figma
Cloud collaboration and full asset library access require paid add-ons
Backed by a much smaller company than venture-funded competitors like Figma
Less brand recognition and community content compared to Figma or Sketch
Real-time multiplayer collaboration is less mature than browser-first competitors

faqs.question: Is Lunacy really free?
faqs.answer: Yes, the core Lunacy desktop editor is free for both personal and commercial use with no major feature restrictions; paid add-ons only cover cloud storage, larger teams, and the full asset and AI library.

faqs.question: Who makes Lunacy?
faqs.answer: Lunacy is developed by Icons8, a design-assets company that also provides the icon, photo, and illustration library integrated directly into the app.

faqs.question: Does Lunacy work offline?
faqs.answer: Yes, unlike browser-based tools such as Figma, Lunacy runs as a native desktop application on Windows, macOS, and Linux and works fully offline.

faqs.question: Can Lunacy open Sketch files?
faqs.answer: Yes, Lunacy natively opens, edits, and saves .sketch files, which was its original purpose when it launched as a Sketch file viewer in 2016.

faqs.question: What AI tools does Lunacy include?
faqs.answer: Lunacy includes an AI background remover, an image upscaler, and an AI avatar generator built directly into the editor.

faqs.question: How does Lunacy compare to Figma?
faqs.answer: Lunacy is a free, native desktop alternative to Figma with offline support and a bundled asset library, though it has a smaller plugin ecosystem and less mature real-time collaboration than Figma.

use_cases.title: Free UI and product design
use_cases.description: Designers use Lunacy to create app and website interfaces with vector tools and prototyping at no cost.

use_cases.title: Cross-platform Sketch file collaboration
use_cases.description: Windows and Linux users open and edit .sketch files created by Mac-based team members using Sketch.

use_cases.title: Offline design work
use_cases.description: Designers who need to work without an internet connection use Lunacy's native desktop app instead of browser-based tools.

features: No limits on deleted file storage
Unlimited Icons8 asset downloads without attribution
Full access to AI design tools
